skill.name: threejs-builder
skill.description: Creates focused Three.js web apps with scene setup, lighting, geometry, materials, animation loops, controls, and responsive rendering patterns.
skill.summary: Builds simple, performant Three.js scenes and interactive 3D web app patterns.
skill.author: CesarAugustusGroB

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found documentation-only Markdown examples rather than executable skill code. The external command detections are false positives caused by Markdown backticks and code fences, while the network findings are expected CDN import examples for Three.js and related libraries. No prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown inline-code spans and fenced HTML, JavaScript, and TypeScript examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These examples are documentation content, not executable skill logic, and no command injection path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside Markdown code examples or inline documentation. There is no Ruby source, shell wrapper, or runtime path that executes the backtick-delimited text.

low_findings.title: Expected CDN Imports in Example Web Apps
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to unpkg CDN imports for Three.js, OrbitControls, GLTFLoader, post-processing modules, Cannon.js, and Stats.js. This creates a normal third-party dependency for generated examples, but it is not evidence of exfiltration or covert networking.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are real URLs, but their semantic context is browser module loading for Three.js examples. No secrets, local files, or user data are sent to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Documented Package Install Command
low_findings.description: The reference file includes an npm install command for production Three.js apps. This is a standard developer instruction rather than automatic command execution, but users should still review package sources before installing dependencies.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly shown inside a Markdown bash block and is not invoked by the skill. It can affect the local environment only if a user chooses to run it.

low_findings.title: High-Risk Keyword Matches Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner reported C2, reconnaissance, weak cryptography, and network-scanning indicators, but review found Three.js shader variables, physics examples, color constants, helpers, and descriptive words. No malicious command-and-control, reconnaissance workflow, cryptographic use, or scanner invocation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited areas shows graphics, physics, color, and debugging documentation. I did not find evidence of C2 behavior, cryptographic operations, host reconnaissance, or network scanning.

content.value_statement: Creating a Three.js app can require many setup decisions before anything appears on screen. This skill provides practical scene, lighting, animation, control, and performance patterns for small 3D web experiences.
actual_capabilities: Creates minimal Three.js HTML apps with ES module imports and responsive renderer setup.
Guides selection of common geometries, materials, lighting types, and color formats.
Provides animation loop patterns for rotation, bobbing motion, mouse tracking, and rendering.
Shows camera control setup with OrbitControls and resize handling.
Covers advanced examples for GLTF loading, shaders, post-processing, physics, raycasting, and sprites.
Highlights common Three.js anti-patterns and performance tips.
limitations: It is documentation guidance, not a packaged Three.js runtime or build tool.
Generated examples may rely on external CDN imports unless adapted to npm packages.
It focuses on simple scenes and prototypes rather than large 3D engines or asset pipelines.
It does not verify browser GPU compatibility or performance on every device.

use_cases.title: Prototype a 3D Landing Scene
use_cases.description: Create a responsive animated WebGL scene for a product, portfolio, or visual showcase.

use_cases.title: Teach Three.js Fundamentals
use_cases.description: Explain scene graphs, cameras, lighting, materials, and animation loops with concise examples.

use_cases.title: Add Interaction to a Demo
use_cases.description: Use OrbitControls, raycasting, sprites, or physics examples to make a small 3D demo interactive.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a single-file Three.js scene with a rotating object, basic lighting, and responsive resizing.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Product Viewer
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Three.js product viewer with OrbitControls, realistic lighting, and a centered main object.

prompt_templates.title: Add Visual Effects
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Three.js scene with particles, post-processing bloom, and smooth animation that remains performant.

prompt_templates.title: Load Advanced Assets
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Three.js app that loads a GLTF model, auto-centers it, adds controls, and handles resize behavior.

output_examples.input: Create a rotating cube scene for a portfolio header.
output_examples.output: A responsive Three.js page with a lit rotating cube, animation loop, camera setup, and resize handling.

output_examples.input: Make a small 3D product viewer with mouse controls.
output_examples.output: A browser scene with OrbitControls, balanced lighting, a centered object, and smooth camera damping.

output_examples.input: Show how to add bloom and particles to a demo.
output_examples.output: A guided implementation plan using reusable geometry, a particle field, and post-processing settings.

best_practices: Start with the scene graph, camera, renderer, and one visible object before adding effects.
Reuse geometries and materials, and cap pixel ratio for better performance on high-density screens.
Choose materials, lighting, and camera controls based on the purpose of the scene.
anti_patterns: Creating new geometries or materials inside every animation frame.
Using legacy global Three.js patterns with modern ES module examples.
Adding effects before confirming the base scene renders correctly.

faq.question: Does this skill create complete Three.js apps?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and examples for complete small apps, including setup, lighting, animation, and resizing.

faq.question: Can it use npm instead of CDN imports?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include npm import paths for Three.js core and addons.

faq.question: Does it support GLTF or GLB model loading?
faq.answer: Yes. The advanced reference includes GLTFLoader setup, model centering, scaling, and camera positioning.

faq.question: Can it help with performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends reuse, InstancedMesh, limited shadow maps, pixel ratio caps, and other practical optimizations.

faq.question: Is it limited to cubes?
faq.answer: No. It covers spheres, cylinders, torus shapes, planes, cones, particles, sprites, shaders, and loaded models.

faq.question: Does it include security-sensitive behavior?
faq.answer: No confirmed malicious behavior was found. The main risks are optional CDN dependencies and a documented npm install command.
